text: hi this is Emily from Miner penguins
like the cold they're built for it and
most live in the cold Waters near
Antarctica but over 4,000 M North in the
tropical Galapagos Islands you can also
find Penguins because of something
called The Cromwell current this cold
water current flows from west to east at
about 100 M below the ocean surface
until it collides with the underwater
part of the gapos islands and turns
upward toward the surface the current
helps the Penguins stay cool but even
more importantly it helps them eat
that's because it brings together
nutrients and sunlight the key
ingredients that make green algae Thrive
which allows penguin's food to thrive
too across most of the ocean there's a
sort of cruel separation between these
ingredients because sunlight only
penetrates the top layer of water while
nutrients sink what's more because the
sunwarm surface waters are less dense
than the colder Waters below the two
layers don't easily mix it takes winds
or a current to bring the nutrient-rich
waters up to the the sunlet surface so
the ocean is like a salad dressing where
the denser vinegar stays trapped below
the oil until you shake it up where the
ocean doesn't get shaken up both green
algae and the critters that eat it are
relatively sparse but where it mixes
like at the Galapagos Islands algae and
the rest of the food chain can Thrive of
course penguins are more common in
colder climates partly because icy
surface waters mix more readily with
cold nutrient-rich Waters below but the
world's cold northern oceans don't have
any Penguins because these flightless
birds evolved in the South and it seems
that although changes in climate over
time may have helped them migrate
northward there's simply too much warm
nutrient po water between them and the
Arctic in fact the world's only Northern
penguins are the Galapagos penguins that
live at the tip of Isabella Island just
a hair or a feather above the equator
[Music]
